context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 2

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Tuesday 20th February, 2024
Days Covered: 03 and 04 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Outlining Proofs [10 points]

See lecture 02 (or the corresponding lecture notes) for a discussion of outlining proofs, even with only partial information. Practice this skill by outlining proofs for each of the "theorems" below as much as you can, using the techniques indicated. Pretend that the underlined words are meaningful but that their precise meanings are not available here. This means your "proofs" will have missing steps that are impossible to complete without definitions for these words, but that's fine—we're interested in the parts of the proof that can be completed even without knowing or considering the underlined terms' meanings. You should break down the proof as granularly as you can using just the given information, and at any step that needs additional information, identify the remaining task or tasks using square brackets. The example seen in lecture looks like this:

    Theorem: "For every integer n, the number n is fooish precisely when n + 1 is barsome."
    Proof Outline. Suppose n is any integer; we must show F(n) and B(n + 1) both imply each other.
    To prove F(n) IMPLIES B(n + 1), first assume F(n) is true. [TODO: prove B(n + 1).]
    To prove B(n + 1) IMPLIES F(n), instead assume B(n + 1) is true. [TODO: prove F(n).]

(a) [3 pts] Theorem: "Every natural number is tall or wide, but not both." (Proof method: proof by cases.)
(b) [3 pts] Theorem: "There exists a happy natural number between 10 and 1000 that has no happy proper divisors." (Proof method to use: choose 25 as the example.)
(c) [4 pts] Theorem: "There are no devious natural numbers." (Proof method to use: regular induction, with base cases 0 and 1.)
    Hint: Be sure to clearly identify your predicate P(n), and to precisely specify any assumptions and/or goals in your base cases and inductive step.


Problem 2. Spotting Errors [15 points]

Define a sequence of integers (x1, x2, x3, ...) as follows: x1 = 4, x2 = 13, and for all i ≥ 3, xi = 6·x_{i−1} − 8·x_{i−2}. So the sequence begins with (x1, x2, x3, x4, ...) = (4, 13, 46, 172, ...). Our goal is to prove that for all i ≥ 1, xi > 0.

Addled Abra tries to prove the claim directly by strong induction with inductive hypothesis P(i) := "xi > 0", but gets stuck in the inductive step: xi+1 = 6·xi − 8·x_{i−1} > 0 − 8·x_{i−1}, "but, uh, ... Huh, I don't think this is working." Abra gets points for honesty, and goes to the instructors for help.

(a) [3 pts] Befuddled Brynmor tries to help by proving the claim using the stronger induction hypothesis Q(i) := "xi ≥ 2^i", via strong induction. His inductive step plugs xi ≥ 2^i and x_{i−1} ≥ 2^{i−1} into xi+1 = 6·xi − 8·x_{i−1} to get xi+1 ≥ 6·2^i − 8·2^{i−1} = 4·2^{i−1} = 2^{i+1}. What is his mistake?

(b) [3 pts] Erroneous Erik tries to help by proving the claim using (regular) induction on the stronger hypothesis R(i) := "xi ≥ 3·x_{i−1}", for every i ≥ 2, with base case R(2): x2 = 13 ≥ 12 = 3·x1. What is his mistake?

(c) [3 pts] Overzealous Zach tries to help by proving the claim using strong induction on the stronger hypothesis S(i) := "xi = 4^i", for every i ≥ 1, with base case S(1): x1 = 4 = 4^1. What is his mistake?
    Hint: Zach's claim is false if i = 2. If you know the base case i = 1, what part of the inductive step when i = 1 fails?

(d) [6 pts] Prove the original claim that for all i ≥ 1, xi > 0.
    Hint: See if you can fix Erroneous Erik's argument. What stronger inductive hypothesis will you use?


Problem 3. Integer Multiplication from Bit Shifts [17 points]

Multiplying and dividing an integer n by 2 requires only a one-bit left or right shift of the binary representation of n, a fast, hardware-supported operation on most computers. For example, 6 = (0110) in binary; multiplying by 2 yields 12 = (1100). And 18 = (10010); dividing by 2 yields 9 = (01001).

We will show that the following state machine computes the product of two nonnegative integers x and y using just these shift operations, along with integer addition:
- The set of states is ℕ^3 = {(r, s, a) | r, s, a ∈ ℕ}.
- The start state is (x, y, 0).
- The transitions are:
    (r, s, a) →  (2r, s/2, a)            for even s > 0,
    (r, s, a) →  (2r, (s − 1)/2, a + r)  for odd s > 0.
- No transitions are defined starting from triples in which s = 0.

We will show that this state machine eventually reaches a final state (the algorithm doesn't run forever), and that this final state (rf, sf, af) has af = x · y.

(a) [2 pts] Compute the full sequence of transitions from starting state (5, 22, 0). Does it correctly compute 5 · 22 = 110?
(b) [4 pts] Invariant: Define P(r, s, a) := "rs + a = xy". Prove that P is an invariant of the state machine.
(c) [1 pt] Final states: A final state is one from which no transitions are possible. Which states are final in this state machine?
(d) [3 pts] Partial correctness: Use Parts (b) and (c) to conclude that, in any reachable final state (rf, sf, af), af must be equal to the product xy.

Now we consider termination. For a natural number n, let bitcount(n) denote the number of bits used when n is written in binary. For example, bitcount(5) = bitcount(101_2) = 3, bitcount(1) = 1, and bitcount(0) = 0. (Note: this can be written explicitly as bitcount(n) = ⌈log2(n + 1)⌉.)

(e) [5 pts] Termination: Prove that bitcount(s) is a strictly decreasing derived variable. Explain why this implies that the algorithm always terminates, i.e., it has no infinite executions.
(f) [2 pts] Time bound: Explain why Part (e) implies that, from any starting state (x, y, 0), the algorithm terminates after at most bitcount(y) = ⌈log2(y + 1)⌉ steps.


Problem 4. Invariant Enlightenment [8 points]

The ideal 6.1200 classroom has assigned desks (each student sits at the same position every day) arranged in a square grid. Students are working together to reach Invariant Enlightenment: a profound understanding of invariants so thorough that the knowledge radiates around them in a glowing aura. Invariant Enlightenment never goes away (it's an invariant, after all!) and can sometimes be taught to other students.

[Figure: a 6×6 grid of seats. The locations of students who have initially been enlightened are marked with an asterisk, scattered across the grid.]

Every day, students talk to their neighbors about invariants in an attempt to reach enlightenment. On each day, a student is enlightened if either:
- the student was already enlightened on a previous day, or
- the student was adjacent to at least two students who were enlightened on an earlier day (these neighbors worked together to teach the student all about invariants).

Here "adjacent" means the students' individual squares share an edge (front, back, left, or right); they are not adjacent if they only share a corner point. So each student is adjacent to 2, 3, or 4 others. [In the example, enlightenment spreads over successive days until all students are enlightened.]

Theorem. If fewer than n students among those in an n × n arrangement are initially enlightened, and additional students reach enlightenment only by being taught by two or more neighbors, then there will be at least one student who never reaches enlightenment.

Prove this theorem.
    Hint: You'll want to find an applicable preserved state predicate or monotonic derived variable that can help you argue about the set of enlightened students as time proceeds. Make sure to prove your claims. If you are stuck, ask at Office Hours or on Piazza for an extremely powerful one-word clue (and even more hints as necessary).

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: uh idk i'm kinda lost on ps2 rn, like all of it feels fuzzy tbh. can we start w whichever part is least bad?
exchanges.tutor: Happy to help you work through it! Rather than me picking for you — which problem did you at least *read* and feel like you somewhat understood what was being asked, even if you couldn't solve it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is overwhelmed and doesn't know where to start. Rather than picking a problem for them, I should guide them to engage with the material themselves by asking them which problem they've spent the most time on or feel least confused about. This respects the Socratic method and keeps the student in the driver's seat intellectually. I'll keep it short and encouraging without being sycophantic.

exchanges.student: maybe problem 2? i read it but the induction stuff all blurred together ngl. i can't tell what each person's mistake even means.
exchanges.tutor: Good choice to start there. Let's focus on part (a) first — Brynmor's attempt. Before we look for the mistake, can you tell me in your own words: what is Brynmor trying to prove, and what proof method is he using?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has chosen Problem 2, which is about spotting errors in induction proofs. Rather than explaining what the mistakes are, I should use the Socratic method to get the student to engage with the problem directly. A good starting point is to ask them to focus on one specific part - let's start with part (a) about Brynmor. I'll ask them to first articulate what Brynmor is actually trying to prove and what steps he takes, to make sure they understand the setup before identifying the error.

exchanges.student: i think he’s doing strong induction to show \(x_i \ge 2^i\) for all i? but i’m already mixed up on why proving a “stronger” thing is allowed fr.
exchanges.tutor: Good, you've got the hypothesis right. On your question about 'why is proving something stronger allowed' — think about it this way: if Q(i) implies P(i) for all i, and you prove Q(i) for all i, what can you conclude about P(i)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified what Brynmor is trying to prove but is confused about why proving a stronger hypothesis is valid. This is a good conceptual question worth addressing briefly, but I shouldn't over-explain - just ask a guiding question that helps them see the logic themselves. The key insight is: if you prove something stronger, the weaker claim follows immediately. I'll use a short Socratic nudge.

exchanges.student: i guess then P(i) is true too since stronger means it includes the original claim? but tbh i still don't see where Brynmor messed up, it kinda looks valid to me.
exchanges.tutor: Right! So now look carefully at what Brynmor actually writes out. What are the required components of a valid strong induction proof, and which of those do you see Brynmor actually doing?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly understood why proving a stronger claim is valid. Now I need to guide them to find Brynmor's actual mistake. The error is in the base cases - Brynmor only checks the inductive step but never verifies the base cases. Strong induction needs base cases, and specifically for this recurrence which depends on two previous terms, you need two base cases. I should ask the student to look carefully at what Brynmor actually does vs. what a complete strong induction proof requires.

exchanges.student: umm base case stuff and inductive step i think? i only really see the step algebra here, so maybe he just never checked the starting values or smth.
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track! Now be more specific: this recurrence uses x_i and x_{i-1} in the inductive step. How many base cases does Brynmor need to check, and which ones are they?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified the issue correctly - Brynmor never checks the base cases. I should affirm this and push them to be more precise: which base cases specifically are missing, and why does this matter for this particular recurrence? The recurrence depends on two previous terms (i and i-1), so you need two base cases. I want the student to articulate this themselves.

exchanges.student: i think 2 base cases, like \(i=1\) and \(i=2\)? but now i'm not sure if one of those already fails lol.
exchanges.tutor: Good — so check it! The problem gives you x_1 = 4 and x_2 = 13. Do both Q(1) and Q(2) actually hold?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified that two base cases are needed. Now I should nudge them to actually check whether Q(1) and Q(2) hold, i.e., whether x_1 ≥ 2^1 and x_2 ≥ 2^2. This is straightforward from the given values x_1=4 and x_2=13. Let the student do the arithmetic themselves.

exchanges.student: yeah \(4 \ge 2\) and \(13 \ge 4\), so both hold... which is why i'm confused again, like what is actually wrong w his proof then?
exchanges.tutor: You've actually found it — Brynmor never explicitly checks or states the base cases in his proof. Even if they happen to hold, a proof that omits them is incomplete. Can you now write a one or two sentence description of his mistake in your own words?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student checked the base cases and found they hold, which is correct. Now they're confused because the base cases are fine and the inductive step algebra also looks correct. The actual mistake is more subtle: Brynmor never explicitly states or verifies the base cases in his proof - he just does the inductive step. But wait, let me reconsider - the problem says 'his inductive step plugs xi ≥ 2^i and x_{i−1} ≥ 2^{i−1}' which means he's using strong induction but the inductive step assumes BOTH Q(i) and Q(i-1). For the very first application of the inductive step (i=1 going to i=2), he needs Q(0) as well - but i starts at 1, so Q(0) isn't defined/proven. Actually, the key issue is simpler: Brynmor never states the base cases at all in what's written. The problem statement only shows his inductive step. So Brynmor's mistake is simply omitting the base cases from his proof. The student has correctly identified this. I should confirm they've found the mistake and ask them to state it cleanly.

exchanges.student: ok wait but that feels kinda too easy?? i’m worried the other parts are gonna have weird hidden gotchas too.
exchanges.tutor: Trust your reasoning — you worked it out logically. Write it up and move on. What do you notice about part (b), Erik's attempt? What is he actually trying to prove and what method is he using?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is second-guessing themselves, which is good critical thinking, but I shouldn't validate or deny whether the answer is 'too easy' - that would be giving away the answer. The base case omission IS the mistake for part (a). I should encourage the student to commit to their answer and move on to part (b) to build momentum, rather than dwelling on part (a).

exchanges.student: i think he’s using regular induction on \(R(i): x_i \ge 3x_{i-1}\), but idk why regular induction is bad here vs strong induction.
exchanges.tutor: Good observation. Don't worry about regular vs. strong induction in the abstract yet — instead, try to actually carry out Erik's inductive step. He wants to prove R(i+1), meaning x_{i+1} ≥ 3x_i. Using the recurrence x_{i+1} = 6x_i − 8x_{i−1} and only assuming R(i), can you complete that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a good question but is perhaps focusing on the wrong thing - the issue isn't necessarily that regular induction is 'bad' per se, but rather whether Erik's inductive step actually works with only one previous value available. In regular induction, the inductive step only gives you R(i), i.e., x_i ≥ 3x_{i-1}, but to prove R(i+1): x_{i+1} ≥ 3x_i using the recurrence x_{i+1} = 6x_i - 8x_{i-1}, you need to know something about x_{i-1} too. Regular induction only gives you the immediately preceding step. I should guide the student to try to actually execute the inductive step and see where it breaks down.

exchanges.student: not really tbh, i keep feeling like i need info about \(x_{i-1}\) too and then everything spirals.
exchanges.tutor: That spiral you're feeling *is* the mistake. Regular induction only gives you R(i) as your hypothesis, but the recurrence requires you to know something about x_{i-1} too. Can you state Erik's mistake in one or two sentences in your own words?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the core issue - you need information about x_{i-1} but regular induction only gives you R(i). This is exactly Erik's mistake. I should affirm this insight and ask them to articulate it clearly as the mistake.
